answer:
**"Tazmeen bar sher-e Aneesi Shamlu" (Chapter 24 of *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*) is an Urdu poem by Allama Iqbal in the form of a *tazmeen*, a traditional poetic device where the poet embeds (or "embroiders") a specific *sher* (couplet) by another poet—in this case, Aneesi Shamlu—into their own composition, often to expand, contrast, or comment upon its theme.[^1][^2]**

The poem narrates a lover's restless wandering ("Like the wandering morning breeze, I live my days"), preferring love's journey to its end, until reaching the shrine of Pir Sanjar (a Sufi figure) for spiritual remedy.[^3] A voice from the grave then rebukes the speaker as a "deserter of the ancestral way," invoking the legendary Qais (Majnun) whose passion for Laila has cooled, despite her enduring allure—symbolizing a betrayal of Islamic spiritual fervor.[^4] It accuses him of failing to let the seed of *La Ilaha* ("There is no god but God") sprout, instead building "foreign temples" filled with "hymns of the church" while raised in the House of God yet devoted to idols.[^5] The embedded *sher* by Shamlu seals the critique:  
*You learned devotion from us, to serve another's art;*  
*You stole a pearl from our hands, to grace another's heart.*[^6]

Overall, the poem critiques cultural and spiritual alienation: a Muslim (or South Asian intellectual) abandoning indigenous Islamic/Sufi roots for Western materialism or Christianity, using romantic allegory to urge reconnection with faith.[^3][^4][^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 24 ("Tazmeen bar sher-e Aneesi Shamlu"), translation (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/translation/#p-e54d95).  
[^2]: The title explicitly signals this form; cf. original (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/original/#p-3a094e).  
[^3]: Ibid., translation (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/translation/#p-33dc81).  
[^4]: Ibid. (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/translation/#p-33dc81).  
[^5]: Ibid. (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/translation/#p-33dc81).  
[^6]: Ibid. (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/translation/#p-ed2ccc); original (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24/original/#p-d71df3).
